claims.evidence_text: The coleoptile, or second leaf, penetrates the soil and results in emergence of the seedling, usually within 5 to 7 days after planting.
claims.claim_summary: Seedling emergence usually within 5-7 days after planting

claims.evidence_text: Seed should have at least 85 percent germination and 56 pounds per bushel test weight and should not be shriveled.
claims.claim_summary: Seed should have at least 56 lb/bushel test weight

claims.evidence_text: Seed should have at least 85 percent germination and 56 pounds per bushel test weight and should not be shriveled.
claims.claim_summary: Seed should have at least 85 percent germination

claims.evidence_text: Most of the roots are in the plow layer, or top 6 inches of soil, but some penetrate as far as 7 feet into the soil.
claims.claim_summary: Roots can penetrate as far as 7 feet into the soil

claims.evidence_text: A winter wheat plant grown under usual conditions will have three to six tillers.
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat plant typically has 3-6 tillers under usual conditions

claims.evidence_text: The growing point is the most susceptible of all plant parts to frost at this stage.
claims.claim_summary: Growing point most frost-susceptible at jointing

claims.evidence_text: Wheat is classified as a long day plant because it will flower only when days are long and nights, the important times, are short.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat is a long-day plant; flowers only under long days

claims.evidence_text: Temperatures only slightly below freezing quickly kill the male flower parts, sterilizing the florets so no grain is formed.
claims.claim_summary: Frost at flowering sterilizes florets, preventing grain set

claims.evidence_text: The maximum weight occurs about 4 weeks after flowering in Kansas. This period is determined largely by temperature and can extend up to 12 weeks in areas where the weather is cool.
claims.claim_summary: Maximum grain weight about 4 weeks after flowering (up to 12 weeks in cool areas)

claims.evidence_text: The kernel contains 30 to 35 percent water at physiological maturity.
claims.claim_summary: Kernel contains 30-35% water at physiological maturity

claims.evidence_text: The most important change is the loss of moisture from 30 to 35 percent in mature grain to 12 to 13 percent in combine-ripe grain.
claims.claim_summary: Combine-ripe grain moisture 12-13%

claims.evidence_text: In recent years, the average grain protein in Kansas wheat has been slightly over 11 percent, which is below the optimum for domestic flour production.
claims.claim_summary: Average Kansas wheat grain protein slightly over 11%

claims.evidence_text: Summer preplant and spring topdressing are the recommended times of nitrogen application for fall seeded small grains in Kansas.
claims.claim_summary: Recommended N timing: summer preplant and spring topdressing

claims.evidence_text: Spring applications of nitrogen should be completed by jointing, which normally occurs in early to mid-March.
claims.claim_summary: Spring N applications should be completed by jointing

claims.evidence_text: N Rec (lbs/a) = [YG × 1.75] STA– PCA– PYM – PNST where, N Rec = nitrogen recommended in pounds per acre YG = a realistic yield goal in bushels per acre
claims.claim_summary: N recommendation = yield goal x 1.75 lb/bu, adjusted for soil/credits

claims.evidence_text: In years of heavy straw production, an additional 20 pounds of nitrogen per ton of incorporated straw should be considered to help decompose the straw
claims.claim_summary: Add 20 lb N per ton incorporated straw to aid decomposition

claims.evidence_text: Excess nitrogen applied early in the season can lead to lodging problems or excess nitrate leaching in some areas and should be approached with caution on fields with a history of lodging.
claims.claim_summary: Excess early-season nitrogen increases lodging risk

claims.evidence_text: Figure 1. Optimum planting dates in Kansas Zone 1.............September 10-30 Zone 2.............September 15-October 20 Zone 3.............September 25-October 20 Zone 4.............October 5-October 25
claims.claim_summary: Optimum Kansas planting dates Sept 10 - Oct 25 by zone

claims.evidence_text: Seeding rates in western Kansas range from 600,000 to 900,000 seeds per acre planted (40 to 60 pounds per acre at a rate of 15,000 seeds per pound)
claims.claim_summary: Western Kansas seeding rate 600,000-900,000 seeds/acre (40-60 lb/acre)

claims.evidence_text: In central Kansas, the seeding rates range from 750,000 to 900,000 seeds per acre (50 to 60 pounds per acre at a rate of 15,000 seeds per pound). Final stands should be 600,000 to 720,000 plants per acre.
claims.claim_summary: Central Kansas seeding rate 750,000-900,000 seeds/acre (50-60 lb/acre)

claims.evidence_text: g rates range from 900,000 to 1,125,000 seeds per acre (60 to 75 pounds per acre at a rate of 15,000 seeds per pound) with final stands of 720,000 to 900,000 plants per acre.
claims.claim_summary: Eastern Kansas seeding rate 900,000-1,125,000 seeds/acre (60-75 lb/acre)

claims.evidence_text: With irrigation, seeding rates may range from 900,000 to 1,350,000 seeds per acre (60 to 90 pounds per acre at a rate of 15,000 seeds per pound) and final stands of 720,000 to 1,080,000 plants per
claims.claim_summary: Irrigated seeding rate 900,000-1,350,000 seeds/acre (60-90 lb/acre)

claims.evidence_text: 80 percent germination and emergence, the plant population may vary from 450,000 to 750,000 plants per acre.
claims.claim_summary: Plant population 450,000-750,000 plants/acre (western Kansas, 80% emergence)

claims.evidence_text: Wheat can emerge from various depths, but a planting depth of 1 to 2 inches is optimal
claims.claim_summary: Optimal planting depth 1-2 inches

claims.evidence_text: Boot to heading may be the critical stage of growth for wheat, although wheat is tolerant of water stress throughout its life cycles.
claims.claim_summary: Boot to heading is the critical stage for water stress

claims.evidence_text: Peak daily water use rates of wheat can approach 0.5 inches per day but normally peak use rates will be around 0.35 inches per day during heading and grain development.
claims.claim_summary: Peak daily water use ~0.35 in/day (can approach 0.5) during heading/grain development

claims.evidence_text: e range of 16 to 24 inches for a wheat crop that is not water limited.
claims.claim_summary: Seasonal water use 16-24 inches for a non-water-limited wheat crop

claims.evidence_text: As the wheat approaches jointing, soil water availability should not be allowed to deplete to less than 50 percent available throughout the remainder of the growing season.
claims.claim_summary: Maintain soil water above 50% depletion from jointing onward

claims.evidence_text: Irrigation management strategies for full yield potential need to include scheduling of irrigation application to maintain soil water above 50 percent depletion.
claims.claim_summary: Schedule irrigation to maintain soil water above 50% depletion

claims.evidence_text: Heavy fall irrigation works well on deep silt and silty clay loams with available water holding capacities of 2.0 inches per feet or more, but fall irrigation would be a failure on sandy soils
claims.claim_summary: Heavy fall irrigation works on soils with AWHC of 2.0 in/ft or more

claims.evidence_text: Wheat should be stored at 13 percent or less moisture content.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat should be stored at 13% or less moisture

claims.evidence_text: Crop rotation is probably the best way to manage these weeds. Rotation to a summer crop helps break the life cycle of these weeds
claims.claim_summary: Rotation to a summer crop recommended to manage winter annual grass weeds

claims.evidence_text: Preharvest sprouting of ripened grain occurs occasionally in eastern and central Kansas when harvest is delayed by wet conditions. Frequent rains, high humidity, and heavy dews cause kernels in the spike to absorb moisture and start germinating.
claims.claim_summary: Preharvest sprouting risk when harvest delayed by wet conditions

claims.evidence_text: Semidwarf wheat varieties form short coleoptiles as well as short plants, and planting depth is particularly critical for them.
claims.claim_summary: Semidwarf varieties form short plants and short coleoptiles
